member.name: T:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.DynamicallyAccessedMembersAttribute
member.summary: Indicates that certain members on a specified are accessed dynamically, for example through .
member.remarks:
This allows tools to understand which members are being accessed during the execution of a program.
This attribute is valid on members whose type is or .
When this attribute is applied to a location of type , the assumption is that the string represents a fully qualified type name.
When this attribute is applied to a class, interface, or struct, the members specified can be accessed dynamically on instances returned from calling on instances of that class, interface, or struct.
If the attribute is applied to a method it's treated as a special case and it implies the attribute should be applied to the "this" parameter of the method. As such the attribute should only be used on instance methods of types assignable to System.Type (or string, but no methods will use it there).

member.name: T:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute
member.summary: /// Suppresses reporting of a specific rule violation, allowing multiple suppressions on a single code artifact.
member.remarks: is different than in that it doesn't have a . So it is always preserved in the compiled assembly.

member.name: M:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.#ctor(System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class, specifying the category of the tool and the identifier for an analysis rule.
member.param: The category for the attribute.
The identifier of the analysis rule the attribute applies to.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.Category
member.summary: Gets the category identifying the classification of the attribute.
member.remarks: The property describes the tool or tool analysis category for which a message suppression attribute applies.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.CheckId
member.summary: Gets the identifier of the analysis tool rule to be suppressed.
member.remarks: Concatenated together, the and properties form a unique check identifier.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.Scope
member.summary: Gets or sets the scope of the code that is relevant for the attribute.
member.remarks: The Scope property is an optional argument that specifies the metadata scope for which the attribute is relevant.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.Target
member.summary: Gets or sets a fully qualified path that represents the target of the attribute.
member.remarks: The property is an optional argument identifying the analysis target of the attribute. An example value is "System.IO.Stream.ctor():System.Void". Because it is fully qualified, it can be long, particularly for targets such as parameters. The analysis tool user interface should be capable of automatically formatting the parameter.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.MessageId
member.summary: Gets or sets an optional argument expanding on exclusion criteria.
member.remarks: The property is an optional argument that specifies additional exclusion where the literal metadata target is not sufficiently precise. For example, the cannot be applied within a method, and it may be desirable to suppress a violation against a statement in the method that will give a rule violation, but not against all statements in the method.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.Justification
member.summary: Gets or sets the justification for suppressing the code analysis message.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.ExperimentalAttribute.DiagnosticId
member.summary: Gets the ID that the compiler will use when reporting a use of the API the attribute applies to.
member.remarks:
The diagnostic ID is shown in build output for warnings and errors.
This property represents the unique ID that can be used to suppress the warnings or errors, if needed.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.ExperimentalAttribute.UrlFormat
member.summary: Gets or sets the URL for corresponding documentation. The API accepts a format string instead of an actual URL, creating a generic URL that includes the diagnostic ID.
member.value: The format string that represents a URL to corresponding documentation.
member.remarks: An example format string is https://contoso.com/obsoletion-warnings/{0}.

member.name: T:System.Runtime.CompilerServices.SkipLocalsInitAttribute
member.summary: Used to indicate to the compiler that the .locals init flag should not be set in method headers.
member.remarks: This attribute is unsafe because it may reveal uninitialized memory to the application in certain instances (e.g., reading from uninitialized stackalloc'd memory). If applied to a method directly, the attribute applies to that method and all nested functions (lambdas, local functions) below it. If applied to a type or module, it applies to all methods nested inside. This attribute is intentionally not permitted on assemblies. Use at the module level instead to apply to multiple type declarations.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Shared.Pools.PoolFactory.CreateCancellationTokenSourcePool(System.Int32)
member.summary: Creates an object pool of instances.
member.param: The maximum number of items to keep in the pool. This defaults to 1024. This value is a recommendation, the pool may keep more objects than this.
member.remarks: On .NET 6 and above, cancellation token sources are reusable and this pool leverages this feature. When running on older frameworks, this pool is actually a no-op, every time a source is fetched from the pool, it is always a new instance. In that case, returning an object to the pool merely disposes it.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience.HttpHedgingStrategyOptions
member.summary: Implementation of the class for results.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience.HttpHedgingStrategyOptions.#ctor
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class.
member.remarks: By default, the options are configured to handle only transient failures. Specifically, this includes HTTP status codes 408, 429, 500 and above, as well as , , and exceptions.

members.member:
Options for the pipeline of resilience strategies for usage in hedging HTTP scenarios.
/// These options represents configuration for 5 chained layers in this order (from the outermost to the innermost):
Total Request Timeout -> Hedging -> Bulkhead (per endpoint) -> Circuit Breaker (per endpoint) -> Attempt Timeout (per endpoint).
The configuration of each resilience strategy is initialized with the default options per type. The request goes through these resilience strategies:
Total request timeout strategy applies an overall timeout to the execution, ensuring that the request including hedging attempts does not exceed the configured limit.
The hedging strategy executes the requests against multiple endpoints in case the dependency is slow or returns a transient error.
The rate limiter pipeline limits the maximum number of requests being send to the dependency.
The circuit breaker blocks the execution if too many direct failures or timeouts are detected.
The attempt timeout strategy limits each request attempt duration and throws if its exceeded.
The last three strategies are assigned to each individual endpoint. The selection of endpoint can be customized by or extensions.
By default, the endpoint is selected by authority (scheme + host + port).

member.name: P:Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience.HttpStandardHedgingResilienceOptions.TotalRequestTimeout
member.summary: Gets or sets the timeout strategy options for the total timeout applied on the request execution.
member.remarks: By default, this property is initialized with a unique instance of using default property values.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience.HttpStandardHedgingResilienceOptions.Hedging
member.summary: Gets or sets the hedging strategy options.
member.remarks: By default, this property is initialized with a unique instance of using default property values.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience.HttpStandardHedgingResilienceOptions.Endpoint
member.summary: Gets or sets the hedging endpoint options.
member.remarks: By default, this property is initialized with a unique instance of using default property values.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience.Internal.RequestMessageSnapshotStrategy
member.summary: This strategy creates a snapshot of before executing the hedging to prevent race conditions when cloning and modifying the message at the same time. This way, all hedged requests will have an unique instance of the message available from snapshot without the need to access the original one for cloning.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience.HttpStandardResilienceOptions
member.summary: Options for resilience strategies for usage in HTTP scenarios.
member.remarks:
These options represent configuration for five chained resilience strategies in this order (from the outermost to the innermost):
Bulkhead -> Total Request Timeout -> Retry -> Circuit Breaker -> Attempt Timeout.
The configuration of each pipeline is initialized with the default options per type. The request goes through these strategies:
Total request timeout pipeline applies an overall timeout to the execution, ensuring that the request including hedging attempts, does not exceed the configured limit.
The retry pipeline retries the request in case the dependency is slow or returns a transient error.
The bulkhead pipeline limits the maximum number of concurrent requests being send to the dependency.
The circuit breaker blocks the execution if too many direct failures or timeouts are detected.
The attempt timeout pipeline limits each request attempt duration and throws if its exceeded.

member.remarks: By default, this property is initialized with a unique instance of using custom of 10 seconds.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience.ResilienceHandlerContext.EnableReloads``1(System.String)
member.summary: Enables dynamic reloading of the resilience pipeline whenever the options are changed.
member.typeparam: The options type to listen to.
member.param: The named options, if any.
member.remarks:
You can decide based on the to listen for changes in global options or named options. If is then the global options are listened to. By default, the parameter is .
You can listen for changes only for single options. If you call this method multiple times, the preceding calls are ignored and only the last one wins.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.ResilienceHttpClientBuilderExtensions
member.summary: Extensions for .
Extensions for .

member.name: M:Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.ResilienceHttpClientBuilderExtensions.AddStandardHedgingHandler(Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.IHttpClientBuilder,System.Action{Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience.IRoutingStrategyBuilder})
member.summary: Adds a standard hedging handler that wraps the execution of the request with a standard hedging mechanism.
member.param: The HTTP client builder.
Configures the routing strategy associated with this handler.
member.returns: A instance that can be used to configure the standard hedging behavior.
member.remarks:
The standard hedging uses a pool of circuit breakers to ensure that unhealthy endpoints are not hedged against. By default, the selection from pool is based on the URL Authority (scheme + host + port). It is recommended that you configure the way the strategies are selected by calling
extensions.
See for more details about the used resilience strategies.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.ResilienceHttpClientBuilderExtensions.AddStandardHedgingHandler(Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.IHttpClientBuilder)

member.param: The HTTP client builder.
member.returns: A instance that can be used to configure the standard hedging behavior.
member.remarks:
The standard hedging uses a pool of circuit breakers to ensure that unhealthy endpoints are not hedged against. By default, the selection from pool is based on the URL Authority (scheme + host + port). It is recommended that you configure the way the strategies are selected by calling
extensions.
See for more details about the used resilience strategies.

member.remarks: The final pipeline name is combination of and . Use pipeline name identifier if your HTTP client contains multiple resilience handlers.
